commentBody:
This felt hard at first but went fast, as it turned out.

I wanted RAzor's EDGE before RAGGED.

Flew through the East, slogged through the Southwest, picked it up in the Northwest. Old fashioned grid with lots of white space. Thanks for letting us fill it in, Daniel. No NCWYETH again today, which is a good sign.
Hard one.
Beauty!

I had to run the alphabet to figure out the crossing of 4D and 17A, though once I had it, 17A made sense. 

This started slow for me, but I seemed to solve it bottom-up. Tough but fair. A beautiful grid: just 4 answers shorter than 5 letters.
31D c'est tres tres bon!  Bravo!
There’s a typo with the capital B in black on 4 down

In his autobiography (Les confessions, 1766), Jean-Jacques Rousseau writes that some time in 1741, he recalled having heard an anecdote about an unnamed princess who was so haughty and out of touch that when she was informed that the starving peasants were clamoring for bread, she replied, "Well, let them eat cake." Marie Antoinette was born in 1755, fourteen years after Rousseau remebered having heard the anecdote — some time before 1741. It was an 18th-century urban legend that was utilized to defame the queen because after falsely accusing her of being a spy, having an affair with a prelate of the church, of incest, and of fraud, her detractors had run out of material. There is also no record of her having uttered the words "Let them eat cake" orally or in writing.
Challenging and fun. Nowhere near as tough as yesterday‘s Friday puzzle. Thanks for the workout.
What's snees?  Nothing what's snees with you?
Breathtaking grid, and the experience was unique, like doing 4 midis. I was puzzling over AMENDMENTI, thinking it was like “literati” 😁Thanks for the aha moment!

I started off with nutellas for spreads out in the morning, which presaged a slower than normal solve. While disappointed that the true answer was SCHMEARS, I was happy to see CHURRO, which would pair very well with Nutella, although best eaten with thick and delicious Spanish hot chocolate, especially in the wee hours of the morning after a night on the town.

With very little connection among the four main quadrants, this one was like four smaller puzzles, and each one posed its own challenges.  The SW was my biggest stumbling block, despite getting ATECAKE right away (great pairing of clue and answer).  SAFARI and ESCARGOT (also great clues) came more slowly.  I saw Back To The Future way back in the past and couldn’t remember OUTATIME, and didn’t know LUISE. The trigger that cracked this corner for me was CLASSPIN. A Saturday gem.

I had "AMEN TO THAT" for 24D and couldn't break out of that frame of mind; ended up DNF'ing because I couldn't be sure INTELSAT was a real company and had never heard of FRISK to mean cavort. Talk about your contronyms...
ROSINED - I bow to thee.
I’m pretty mad at everyone who thought this grid was a good idea — even before they put SNEES and AMENDMENTI in it. (I still don’t know what those are, and I’m not going to look them up.)
Excellent puzzle untainted by Oreos or epees. Let's have more like this.
Amendmeti?? At least this one made more sense than yesterday's. Still not a good one.

Doable yet awkward and uninspired.  Snees?  Looked it up after solving - both British and obsolete!  Not an appropriate crossword answer for the NY Times.  (The author admits to rolling through automated means to construct this, for some reason.  Constructor brownie points or something.  Unique entries; no one cares.)

So very much unimpressed.  Maybe this is one of those constructors whose work is always this tortured?  I don't keep track.  Cavort as frisk?  Dearie me?  Come on.  The Times can do better than this.  It certainly has in the past.

At least it was a bit more challenging that the new norm, I guess.  Sigh.

That NW corner was really tough: MUNSHI and ELSTON side by side, intersecting with SCHMEARS/ UHAULVAN/ BURNSAGE/ ROSINED, plus I had never heard of RAGGED EDGE or HAREMS  being used that way.  Plus having to pull SNEES out of the very back corners of my mind.  I finally got through, but that was some workout!
Tough enjoyable puzzle - took me 01:12:48, but solved it with no lookups.

Oh, never shall I
Forget the cry
Or the shriek that shrieked he
As I gnashed my teeth
When from its sheath
I drew my snickersnee!
— W.S. Gilbert, The Mikado

Definitely one for the constructors rather than the solvers.
NW was just impossible for me in the end - never heard of SCHMEARS, ELSTON, RAGGED EDGE, MUNSHI, BURN SAGE or SNEES. Got the rest with a lot of trial and error, but I personally found it just on the wrong side of the challenging/frustrating line.
Thanks though Dan, certainly an impressive grid.

THIC (Too Hard I Cheated)

I did not like the northwest corner at all. Sorry. No no no. I was sure of CHURROS and HAREMS, while SUBPAR and AVAILED and ROSINED were difficult but fair enough. Not so RAGGED EDGE. This is about tenth down the list of metaphors for what people close to failure are on (starting with thin ice, last roll of the dice, last chance, razor’s edge, knife’s edge, slippery slope, etc.) UHAULVAN and ELSTON and BURN SAGE were ungettable for us foreigners. But SCHMEARS? MUNSHI? SNEES? I don’t mean to be rude but… Are these even words?
Great fun

This took me about an hour. Forty-five minutes of that was in the Northwest. I lost track of how many times I put in and then took out UHAULVAN. Yikes, I’m glad to be done. It was a very satisfying effort.

Ce n'est pas du fast food?

Est-ce vraiment?

Non. N'as-tu pas vu cet escargot? Vroom vroom!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Just short of an hour of mind-flaying fun.

Weak Sauce and I got what we wished for.

Did it in good time (for me, anyway). I was remembering Zapata's first name as "Emilio," which wasn't long enough, so I looked that up. Nothing else, though.

The only reason I knew CHURROS was from watching "The Brothers Sun" on Netflix, and HAREM was a scream-and-leap guess.
I really, really loved the clue for 31 down (ESCARGOT). Best clue ever!

Wow this is one for the ages.  Haven’t seen this much white space since…uh, the movie Frozen?
Was really looking forward to an incredibly difficult puzzle. Certainly got it with the NW and was expecting three more corners of the same. The last three went pretty smoothly though but no complaints, had enough brain cells burnt out already and got some delightful clues like AMENDMENTI and ESCARGOT, which lit up my brain cells again.
Was thinking of the process of figuring out the NW which seemed insurmountable at first. Had UHAULVANS and CHURROS only to start. Was led astray with NOTCHED before ROSINED and thinking about some sort of shirt pockets for T slots. Here’s where letter patterns help. The D of DEEMS suggest the E and then the D of the bow clue, which then suggests ED at the end of AVAILED and RAGGED. (Educated guesses, but still). The seal clue probably ends in an S, so get rid of NOTCHED. OK, HAREMS was a wild flash in the pan guess, admittedly (just thinking about groups of females) but once you have that the RM suggests ARMHOLE and the CH suggests an S for SCHMEARS, then SU — SUBPAR and so on. Newer solvers take note, these tricks actually help.
Everyone, understand the magnitude of this achievement. I know you have to deal with MUNSHI and BURNSAGE and SNEES in that corner. But by rights the whole grid should have had a lot of fill like this for this open a puzzle, and the fact that it didn’t and was so smooth is an immense achievement. Cherish the moment.

commentBody: Today's Maxi calved a Mini.

Little by little, the NE, SE, SW filled in nicely, but the NW was jammed with stuff I didn't know.  I was close to ditching ARMHOLES, which I liked, and SNEES, which seemed too far out, but luckily I didn't.  I finally gave up and looked up the clerk and the ballplayer, to confirm what I had and they gave me the rest. I'd been wavering on two different bows, and had forgotten the third. Even after it was filled in, ROSINED drew a momentary blank.

Three quarters of the puzzle were challenging, and the NW was would have been only that too, but I wonder how many other people had to get help.

I'm glad you broke a record, Dan Bodily, but you may have crashed some others.

Hey Respect the SCHMEAR!
Look up the videos. I would have linked to them but most have some colorful language.
It’s a real thing though.

For me, the SE corner was straight out of a Monday or Tuesday, and the NE one - a Wednesday.

However, the NW corner wiped the floor with me, even thought UHAULVAN and CHURRO were gimmes. MUNSHI, ELSTON, SNEES, ROSINED were all stumpers, and I got BURN [SAGE] and SCHMEARS only once one or two letters were missing, after I had looked up, checked and revealed my total unknowns there.

The SW was impossible for me, too. The gimmes of ATE CAKE and IM HOME were of little help. Quaint American oaths always stump me - there are just so many of them that they can be pretty much anything, effectively. I found the clue for ESCARGOT very vague - not fast food in French? Again, that can be anything. OUTATIME and LUISE were arcana, for me, and I was not familiar with PIGGED, either. As for KINDER, we have those egg things over here, too - but we simply call them "jajko z niespodzianką" (egg with a surprise), so the American, brand-based name was no instant gimme.

This was probably a worthy Saturday puzzle, but it featured too many personal unknowns to be enjoyable for me.

I saw all that white space when the puzzle loaded and groaned out loud…. I solved BL, TR, BR, and finally TL  in just over half my Saturday average which really surprised me.  Somehow I knew SNEES and had UHAULVAN early on but the TL corner still took a bit of work.  

Good puzzle!

Thanks @Patricia Henry and @Steven M. for explaining AMENDMENT I below. AMENDMENTI looked loco and I had no idea how to parse it. https://www.nytimes.com/shared/comment/4gk8nm?rsrc=cshare&smid=url-share
I haven't read any comments yet, but I will say right now that I want my obituary to mention my having solved this puzzle.

Really not a fan of that NW corner. I think that is the most challenging, not in a good way, corner, I have seen in the last 5 years. The layout (no way in) didn't help. 
MUNSHI is just bad fill. ELSTON - know it or unguessable. HAREMS was brutally clued. And for goodness sake, if you're going to use SCHMEARS, at least throw us a bone and imply the Yiddish origin in the hint. 
I know I know, nobody likes a whiner. But there's gotta be some room here for criticism when it's called for. That was awful.

Tough tough but on the whole enjoyable… SNEES never heard of n was wondering if it should be EPEES as I had the two ‘E’s …. It’s weird to have the Mondayesque clue of KINGSIZE in an otherwise quite tough solve. 
 SCHMEAR was annoying. WAYFARER was nice as WANDERER was the obvious answer. 
Enjoyed this… on the whole 😉

I won’t complain about fill I didn’t know. That’s on me.
I can’t object to the challenge. I took it on.
I can and do wish for more… sparkle? Wordplay with a little more humor?
This one felt like drudgery for drudgery’s sake. I finished it, but I did not enjoy it.

I completed it with no lookups so I’m feeling rather pleased that I persevered. It took for b… ever. It felt like the absence of black squares was so pleasing to the setter/editors that the clueing took a back foot. 

I believe the rule should be that the setter shouldn’t lookup any words they use in the puzzle. If this was the case then chapeau 

A battle of wits is fine, but in a computer assisted puzzle (as the setter owns up to) it shouldn’t end as a battle of dictionary knowledge. Pitting myself against a machine I don’t need to pay for. 

Others have whinged about the upper left already. It was dreadful.  The rest of the puzzle was ok.
I'm now convinced that I'm not good enough to complete difficult NYT crosswords.  I had to look up almost the entire NW section.  I think I'm done.

Is there a crossword stat for "average" or "mean" answer length.

This puzzle terrified me from the start, because there were so many 5+ letter words.

I feel like my ability to get a no-cross answer goes as the factorial of the length of the word.  So much beyond five, I take a quick glance at the clue, and if something doesn't immediately come to mind, I go on.   

That doesn't work so well when there are so few three and four letter answers.

Thank goodness for ELSTON Howard.
Not a fan. I am all for making the Saturday crossword puzzle more difficult as it has gotten easier in my opinion over the past few years. But the upper left corner of this puzzle was Natick central.
Solved in record time but what is amendmenti?

I really enjoyed this one. Aside from the technical ambition and cosy aesthetic, the cluing sent me down some interesting rabbit holes (and oceanic depths frequented by elephant seals), and "Ce ne pas du fast food" is cute.
I was misled for a while (solving downs first) because 'moguls' also fit in 41D and is factually correct. If that's an intentional misdirect it's a good one!

I love opening a Saturday and seeing a white expanse. Pleasantly tough with the first pass yielding only a few answers but on the second and third, everything fell into place. Escargots was my favorite today!

It was all going so well. I breezed through all but the NW corner, but then hit a roadblock. 7D was my downfall; I first tried AKNIFEEDGE, then RAZORSEDGE, then conceded defeat and peeked.

p.s. I cast an emphatic NO vote for the new route to Wordplay and comments. Most annoying!
Churros are not Mexican

Fun one! Came in a little under my average, but had to erase so many answers I was initially certain about. Had to flyspeck MUNSHe/ROSeNED, but didn’t take too long to find it. The grid design actually made it really easy to scan through and find the mistake. More like this, please!

Lovely, stiff puzzle IMNSHO.

31D was worth the price of admission.
Enjoyed this! First pass gave me anxiety but it all started to fill in with further passes, and even the more obscure trivia was deducible from crosses. Altogether a solid Saturday brain teaser.
Churros are not Mexican, availed does not mean ‘proved useful’…I could go on…

Took the same time as yesterday to dig out from this blizzard.

Masterfully constructed.

Nice achievement on this imposing looking grid, Daniel.  Doing it with a junk free grid is very impressive.

I just love clues like "Like bows, at times".  I was thinking of bowing down or tying a bow, but, no, it was a third kind of bow.  A real D'oh! moment when it emerged.

My thanks to Magritte for helping me interpret the clue for 31 D.  Very funny.

Now, where do I go for freedom *from* religion?
Have gone through comments but haven't found an explanation of "snees?"
Enjoyably challenging!
Great fun. If opening this puzzle and seeing that grid doesn't get your attention, well then...

"Happy Birthday U.S.A.! The American experiment has made it to a quarter millennium.'

That is really debatable right now.

Very crunchy. I almost gave up hope in both the NW and SW corners, but a second cup of coffee did the trick. This is the exact kind of puzzle I like on a Saturday...one in which I teeter on the RAGGEDEDGE of failure.

Impressive puzzle. NW corner took me as much as the rest of the puzzle. The MUNSHI/ELSTON/ROSINED combination baffled me for quite a while, had to improvise a bit, but when that "I" got in place, it felt quite victorious!

Well yes.  This was a feat puzzle, with its seas of white, its remarkably low 60 words and 26 black squares, its 22 longs and a mere two 3s.  With its elegant debut grid design.

But, IMO, a feat puzzle doesn’t count for much if it provides a lousy solve.  For me, it did not do that.  It made me work hard in many places, and my brain takes pleasure in that – in figuring, configuring, throwing in stabs, throwing in bits of answers, working on riddles behind the scenes while working on other riddles in the moment.

Then there was that instant that I actually smelled burning sage though none was here.  And another where I similarly tasted a churro. There was the inner LOL at [Ce n’est pas du fast food].  There were exclamation-point yesses that accompanied filling in many squares, and the satisfaction that came from revisiting long-unthought-of friends such as ELSTON and OUTATIME.

So yes, a feat, but a most worthy one.  Thank you, Daniel, for this impressive and satisfying creation!

Did I just..?

Did I just get *four*…

*FOUR*?

Daniel Bodily Saturday puzzles?

Cuz, I feel like I did.

I’m pretty sure I did.

I did.
And I loved them all.


Mr. Bodily - and I mean this literally -

Wow. Za.
This was really good! I thought it was going to be impossible at first but things just started to fit. Had to go to Wordplay for help in the NW corner but I often have to on Saturdays.

Puzzle of the week!
Possibly more.

If that don't knock the fleas off a dog, well, you better check for signs of life!

Better fill than anything you get in a dentist's chair.

Happy weekend!

Looking at the grid before I started was the first turnoff. 

Those who had difficulty in the NW and solve on a device have an edge (and not a RAGGED EDGE) that we who solve on paper with a gel pen don't have. I don't have the luxury of trying different letter combinations. Sure, I could use an erasable pen, but my style is to just go over an incorrect letter.

For that reason, the NW did me in. Oh, there were some gimmes, due to my advanced age and longtime solving. UHAULVAN and CHURRO and ELSTON and SNEES were gimmes. MUNSHI, SUBPAR, BURN SAGE and HAREMS were not. And who uses SCHMEARS in the plural? Yeah, I know someone will say that they go into a bagel store and order two bagels with two schmears. But it's just wrong.

Using things like computer code to come up with an arcane answer like MUNSHI or a ridiculous one like DEARIE ME is enough to turn me off to the entire puzzle, which it did. For me this was a slog, even with all the other gimmes like EMILIANO, LUISE, and others.
My first thought looking at that grid was that is was a QR code for "www.streak-breaker.com".
100% worth the difficulty for 31D. I am still tickled!
MUNSHIs aren’t necessarily Hindus.  Many are Muslims.  Given the Arabic/Persian origin of the word, it may be arguable a MUNSHI is less Hindu than a CHURRO is Mexican.

Feels like Bodily harm was done me this morning. But ..,

Congratulations!
You solved a Saturday
Crossword in 48:19.

Didn't think I'd manage to solve this one, and the NW took a long time. But got it solved without assistance. Just slowly.

"Ce n'est pas du fast food" for ESCARGOT was a real winner. Maybe more in a later post, once I recover.

Brilliant construction!
What a challenging (and satisfying) grid. I thought of it as four independent midis. And oh how I chuckled when I finally figured out ce qui n'est pas du fast food.

Whew. Typical tough Saturday for me, and had to look up a couple of things, but just made for a nice enjoyable workout. One answer history search today was for: BODILYHARM. And, nope - never been in a puzzle. Appropriate puzzle find today was a Thursday from June 6, 2019 by Fred Piscop. Some answers in that one: OMAHASTEAKS JUNOPROBE SWORDDANCER UTAHSTATE GOLDORE And then the reveal: "It's beaches begin 5 answers in this puzzle" DDAY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/6/2019&h=59d ....

I love the puzzles when at first reading of the across clues (I usually run through these first) the puzzle seems unsolvable to you. On first reading of the acrosses today, I got only 2 off the bat. Looked like it was all over from the start. Then working through the downs, the whole world is slowly revealed and you realise you can do it….  I try to get through the whole week doing all the puzzles on less than 30 minutes per day. Only managed it about 3 times. Today’s beat me by 1 minute 30, but at the beginning today I thought I was toast (no SCHMEAR)!  Happy to lose the weekly challenge on such a great puzzle!

I concur with all of you that this was a devilishly satisfying puzzle (that grid!). But I must offer kudos to the Wordplay blog for leading with a photograph of young Jalen Brunson when he was playing for Villanova's championship team. Now he is leading the New York Knicks in a title run that is truly one for the ages. New Yorkers are kvelling, with outdoor watch parties popping up all over the city as the 53-year title drought appears to be coming to an end. Go KNICKS!

T slots,  armholes?

Anyone?

Why on earth does the column not explain what a SNEE is?

I found the NW corner to be unpleasantly difficult. It was crammed full of esoteric knowledge: groups of female elephant seals, a Hindu cleric, a baseball player from more than half a century ago, and whatever the heck a SNEE is. I had UHAUL VAN, CHURRO, and ARM HOLES filled in, and still had to look up multiple answers.

The rest of the puzzle felt far too easy, with clues that are too direct for a Saturday: the former CBS procedural, aquarium device, the mattress clue. These are all disappointingly straightforward for a Saturday. The only interesting clue was the one for ESCARGOT.
I could not have enjoyed that less. Bummer.
Didn't like this one. TL corner was difficult in the annoying way - lots of obscure trivia (Hindu clerks? Female Elephant seal packs? First black Yankee? Whatever a "Snee" is?)

When I was 20, I went to Teaneck NJ with a college friend.  She had to stop by her aunt's house, since her uncle had recently died.

I still remember the wall of photos documenting an amazing baseball career, and hearing the stories of my friend's beloved uncle, Elston Howard.

A die-hard Pirates fan, I had only vaguely heard of Mr. Howard, but I knew immediately I was in the home of one of the greats of baseball.

I guess some records were never meant to be broken.

Congrats to the constructor on his achievement, however, it came at the expense of the grid (and thus the solving process). This was essentially four mini puzzles with the flimsiest of middle sections tying them together. 

I had solved all corners but the (horrendous) NW. Solving 75% of the puzzle contributed exactly two letters to that corner. Just unacceptable.
It’s funny how you can guess the probable age and crossword-solving experience of a commenter by whether or not he or she is familiar with the word “SNEE.”
That northwest corner was diabolical!
Wow. Nice Saturday for Saturday! Loved the grid, but it really made for four almost separate puzzles, and the NW kicked my butt. Thanks for that!

The NW really kicked my butt. Probably spent ten minutes spinning my wheels there until I made an educated guess that 21A probably started with ARM (I already had CHURRO). The rest pretty quickly fell into place.

Oh, and I had ESCARGOT for dinner (along with duck a la orange, creme brulee, and the better part of a bottle of Burgundy...)

Overall liked this puzzle for a Saturday, and especially how a couple words had consonants and vowels in unexpected places and combinations:  NCISLA, AMENDMENTI, SUPERG.

The last 10 minutes came down to me checking the NW and the SW.  I had SNEES, which I'd never heard of, and cETTER, which didn't seem right but all the crosses looked fine.  NETTER makes sense in retrospect, but I kept thinking of a lepidopterist in terms of mounting, not catching.  I finally googled ELSTON and OUTATIME to make sure those were right, and then I just started going letter by letter until I got it.

Was I the only one with CLASSPIc instead of CLASSPIN?
Fresh and fun.

I'm exhausted, but I did finish it without cheating.  Very slowly and with a lot of suffering.  In this kind of grid design, when you're completely flummoxed in one section, there's no other section that's going to bail you out.  Each quadrant is almost completely self-contained.

BURNS SAGE has been in puzzles before, but I didn't remember it.  Who knew MUNCHI?  Bet you didn't.  Don't know one Mexican fried food from another.  RAGGED EDGE is the particular failure you face when your Green Paint brand isn't selling.  

To make a verb out of ROSIN is ridiculous.  The less said about this, the better.

Thank heaven for you, ELSTON!  You're the only reason I was able to finish the NW.  Well, that and SCHMEARS.  I needed as many crosses to get SCHMEARS as anyone else, but at least I knew what it was, once it came in.

NE problems:  ICE AXE kept me from seeing a lot of the long Downs in the NE.  Thank heaven for you, MOMALA.  I remembered eventually -- though I needed the MO.

SW problems: ANTE IN before STIR IN.  Has anyone said DEARIE ME in the last 200 years?But great clues for ESCARGOT and SAFARI.

SE corner:  The easiest corner for me by far -- with no writeovers.  But what on earth is INTELSAT?

I didn't find this much fun, to tell the truth, but I don't doubt that it did wonders for my little gray cells.

Northwest corner was a beast for me even with 4 clues in Wordplay (which I do not usually read until I am done but needed to today). I found the other 3 quadrants to be fun and rewarding. Wish I thought of Schmears since assorted cream cheese spreads did not fit!

For a Saturday, I found this unusually solvable for me. Of course, my first time through it, I almost gave up. I only filled in, I think, four squares on my first go through. But bit by bit, I trusted myself, and got there. SNEE was new for me, even though I am old. My favorite, of all of them, was the clue for ESCARGOT. That will be my chuckle for the day. A Saturday finished in under 30 minutes is unusual for me, so it was satisfying on many levels. I did not find the NW is hard as some commenters did (I did expect to see some groans about SCHMEAR; surprised to see more about ELTON!). For me, the SE was tougher.

Good luck to those still solving!
I learned SNEE reading the Dune books 50 years ago.

There’s some pretty rough fill in here, but also an absolute gem: ESCARGOT

(btw I’m happy overall — the upper-left corner put up a fun fight!)

Maybe this is a bad analogy, but here goes:
I pay money to go see the Dali museum. Most I Iike, some I don’t. And some I think I understand, and many I could stare at for hours wondering what the meaning to all the parts are. But they are beautiful and exciting and even the ones I don’t understand mesmerize me, and I certainly don’t fault the museum or Dali for not explaining the whole piece or only displaying the ones I like. And I certainly would pay to keep coming back. (Unless they start displaying other artists who bore me). 

Welcome to my crossword experience. I get it, for some of you if you wanted to pay to see Dali you would and instead you are paying to solve a crossword and when you can’t (or to be fair, even if you can but it seems too obscure or obtuse) it irritates you. Fair enough, different strokes. Just wanted to share my perspective.

I DEEM this to be an impressive construction with those heavy blocks held together by DRE and his GANG. 
To begin, I knew my CHURRO and UHAULVAN but not much else so moved on. Thankfully, I found the other corners easier. ESCARGOT was excellent. CIORNCRIB and KINDER egg are new words to me.
If you didn't know BURN SAGE. go to a powwow and learn all about it.  It's for purification.  You can do your own smudging.  I grow my own sage for it.  Sage is a perennial. It grows back every year.
31 down. Best crossword clue ever!
Lovely. ESCARGOT and AMENDMENTI especially. Thank you, Daniel!
Amazing puzzle! More from Dan Bodily, please!

Oof that was rough. RAzorSEDGE had me tripped up for a long time and I had ROSetED for bows. Which was probably spelled wrong but I was stumped.

I like a hard puzzle, just not a fan of the corners not interlocking. But I do like to fight with a Saturday so this one gave me a run for my money!

I see I'm quite late to the party.  I was delayed getting to the puzzle (which may have been just as well.)  A small domestic disaster--a large pot left on the low burner all night (cooktops for old people should have an indicator light to show if any burners are ON)--slowed me down...

The NE was deceptively simple; then the SE followed suit--nary a pause!  Aaaaaand then the YEARBOOK at 28D caused a lot of trouble.  I have never heard of a CLASS PIN; just a CLASS RING.  (KINDER egg?  In the years I spent in West Germany as a child, and later as an adult, I never saw this.  Oh, well.  Maybe it's Austrian? Or Swiss?)

And finally I reached the NW, the Sanctum-Flanktum, one might say.  For a very long time, it was just "The SNEE and I," along with EDGE dangling there at the passage-way. 
I was sure of WALLAH--as in, Box-wallah, etc., in The Raj Quartet which we all read in the wake of the PBS series, right?  I have much experience of UHAULVANs, but took it back out.  JELLIES didn't work, nothing worked!  I was truly tempted to give up or at least LOOK up!  I was wearing out the FriXion eraser!  Oh, the tribulation!

I finally finished it.  SCHMEARS.  Not part of our lives.  Jam, jelly, preserves, honey, and real butter.  Period.

CORN CRIB — Back in the day I bought a DVD of “Old Yeller” and intended to watch it with the four littles at home with popcorn, a blanket, and a box of tissues to get them through it.

Unbeknownst to me, they took it with them one weekend and as I was driving to the camp ground, I heard it come on the video player we had in the minivan. Needless to say, they were all sobbing and howling after the CORN CRIB scene, and we made a quick detour for an ice cream and hanky stop.
This is a divine puzzle in every aspect. What a pleasure. Thanks!

I felt a bit chagrined that so many thought the clue for the first Black player for the Yankees was unfair. ELSTON Howard was a great player, voted to the All-Star Game 12 times, and was on many of the great world champion Yankees teams of the 50s and 60s, playing alongside Mantle, Maris, Berra, and the rest of the dynasty. 

He broke the color barrier for the Yankees in 1955, a full seven years *after* Jackie Robinson did the same for Major League Baseball.  He eventually became their starting catcher, taking over from the great Yogi Berra. That could not have been easy to fill those shoes, 

ELSTON Howard was not only a star player, but also a strong team leader. His number 32 was retired by the Yankees in 1984. 

From Wikipedia, upon his death:

Red Smith, a columnist for The New York Times, reacted by writing, "The Yankees' organization lost more class on the weekend than George Steinbrenner could buy in 10 years."

If you’re constructing a puzzle, *any* attempt to make a particular entry difficult will involve targeting some kinda gap in “the average solver’s” knowledge base (whether of language, terminology, or trivia), deliberately introducing ambiguity, and/or attempting to mislead/misdirect them.

But whatever you do, *some* solvers will instantly get any given answer, and thereby get a leg up on all the crossing entries. This is why the “wavelength” factor, lameness of the term notwithstanding, is decidedly a thing.

Personally, I had no clue about MUNSHI or ELSTON, so I had to treat those as black (or perhaps dark gray) boxes and reason about what *felt* like plausible answers given the clues — and those were both tough ones to evaluate via the “does that look like a real thing” sniff test!

And CHURRO was also a gimme for me (as someone who grew up in Houston, TX, enjoying churros at the Astrodome), but I’ve seen a couple commenters highlight that one as being totally opaque to them.

The corner is a great example of a kind of Friday/Saturday puzzle style that I personally enjoy, in which interlocking trivia and ambiguity/under-specification require incrementally building up a set of crosses as a group (as opposed to puzzling over individually tricky entries in isolation).

Tough? Sure. But that’s a feature, not a bug.
